A.4
Skill Library
A.4.1
Components in the Prompt
The input prompt to GPT-4 consists of the following components:
(1) Guidelines for code generation: See Sec A.4.2 for the full prompt;
(2) Control primitive APIs implemented by us: These APIs serve a dual purpose: they demon-
strate the usage of Mineflayer APIs, and they can be directly called by GPT-4.
• exploreUntil(bot, direction, maxTime = 60, callback): Allow the agent
to explore in a fixed direction for maxTime. The callback is the stopping condition
implemented by the agent to determine when to stop exploring;
• mineBlock(bot, name, count = 1): Mine and collect the specified number of
blocks within a 32-block distance;
• craftItem(bot, name, count = 1): Craft the item with a crafting table nearby;
• placeItem(bot, name, position): Place the block at the specified position;
• smeltItem(bot, itemName, fuelName, count = 1): Smelt the item with the
specified fuel. There must be a furnace nearby;
24


• killMob(bot, mobName, timeout = 300):
Attack the mob and collect its
dropped item;
• getItemFromChest(bot, chestPosition, itemsToGet): Move to the chest at
the specified position and get items from the chest;
• depositItemIntoChest(bot, chestPosition, itemsToDeposit):
Move to
the chest at the specified position and deposit items into the chest;
(3) Control primitive APIs provided by Mineflayer:
• await bot.pathfinder.goto(goal): Go to a specific position. See below for how
to set the goal;
• new GoalNear(x, y, z, range): Move the bot to a block within the specified
range of the specified block;
• new GoalXZ(x, z): For long-range goals that don’t have a specific Y level;
• new GoalGetToBlock(x, y, z): Not get into the block, but get directly adjacent
to it. Useful for fishing, farming, filling a bucket, and using a bed.;
• new GoalFollow(entity, range): Follow the specified entity within the specified
range;
• new GoalPlaceBlock(position, bot.world, {}): Position the bot in order to
place a block;
• new GoalLookAtBlock(position, bot.world, {}): Path towards a position
where a face of the block at position is visible;
• bot.isABed(bedBlock): Return true if bedBlock is a bed;
• bot.blockAt(position): Return the block at position;
• await bot.equip(item, destination): Equip the item in the specified destina-
tion. destination must be one of “hand”, “head”, “torso”, “legs”, “feet”, “off-hand”;
• await bot.consume(): Consume the item in the bot’s hand. You must equip the
item to consume first. Useful for eating food, drinking potions, etc.;
• await bot.fish(): Let bot fish. Before calling this function, you must first get to a
water block and then equip a fishing rod. The bot will automatically stop fishing when
it catches a fish;
• await bot.sleep(bedBlock): Sleep until sunrise. You must get to a bed block
first;
• await bot.activateBlock(block): This is the same as right-clicking a block in
the game. Useful for buttons, doors, etc. You must get to the block first;
• await bot.lookAt(position): Look at the specified position. You must go near
the position before you look at it. To fill a bucket with water, you must look at it first;
• await bot.activateItem(): This is the same as right-clicking to use the item in
the bot’s hand. Useful for using a bucket, etc. You must equip the item to activate first;
• await bot.useOn(entity): This is the same as right-clicking an entity in the game.
Useful for shearing a sheep. You must get to the entity first;
(4) Retrieved skills from the skill library;
(5) Generated code from the last round;
(6) Environment feedback: The chat log in the prompt;
(7) Execution errors;
(8) Critique from the self-verification module;
(9) The agent’s current state: See Sec. A.3.1 for each element of the agent’s state;
(10) Task proposed by the automatic curriculum;
(11) Task context: We prompt GPT-3.5 to ask for general suggestions about how to solve the
task. In practice, this part is handled by the automatic curriculum since it has a systematic
mechanism for question-answering (Sec. A.3.2);
(12) Chain-of-thought prompting [46] in response: We ask GPT-4 to first explain the reason why
the code from the last round fails, then give step-by-step plans to finish the task, and finally
generate code. See Sec. A.4.2 for the full prompt.

A.5
Self-Verification
A.5.1
Components in the Prompt
The input prompt to GPT-4 consists of the following components:
(1) The agent’s state: We exclude other blocks that are recently seen and nearby entities from the
agent’s state since they are not useful for assessing the task’s completeness. See Sec. A.3.1
for each element of the agent’s state;
(2) Task proposed by the automatic curriculum;
(3) Task context: We prompt GPT-3.5 to ask for general suggestions about how to solve the
task. In practice, this part is handled by the automatic curriculum since it has a systematic
mechanism for question-answering (Sec. A.3.2);
(4) Chain-of-thought prompting [46] in response: We request GPT-4 to initially reason about
the task’s success or failure, then output a boolean variable indicating the task’s outcome,
and finally provide a critique to the agent if the task fails.
(5) Few-shot examples for in-context learning [36–38].

B
Experiments
B.1
Experimental Setup
Our simulation environment is built upon MineDojo [23] and utilizes Mineflayer [52] JavaScript APIs
for motor controls (Sec. A.4.2). Additionally, we incorporate many bot.chat() into Mineflayer
functions to provide abundant environment feedback and implement various condition checks along
with try-catch exceptions for continuous execution. If the bot dies, it is resurrected near the closest
ground, and its inventory is preserved for uninterrupted exploration. The bot recycles its crafting table
and furnace after program execution. For detailed implementations, please refer to our codebase.
B.2
Baselines
ReAct [29] uses chain-of-thought prompting [46] by generating both reasoning traces and action
plans with LLMs. We provide it with our environment feedback and the agent states as observations.
ReAct undergoes one round of code generation from scratch, followed by three rounds of code
refinement. This process is then repeated until the maximum prompting iteration is reached.
Reflexion [30] is built on top of ReAct [29] with self-reflection to infer more intuitive future actions.
We provide it with environment feedback, the agent states, execution errors, and our self-verification
module. Similar to ReAct, Reflexion undergoes one round of code generation from scratch, followed
by three rounds of code refinement. This process is then repeated until the maximum prompting
iteration is reached.
AutoGPT [28] is a popular software tool that automates NLP tasks by decomposing a high-level goal
into multiple subgoals and executing them in a ReAct-style loop. We re-implement AutoGPT by
using GPT-4 to do task decomposition and provide it with the agent states, environment feedback,
and execution errors as observations for subgoal execution. Compared with VOYAGER, AutoGPT
lacks the skill library for accumulating knowledge, self-verification for assessing task success, and
automatic curriculum for open-ended exploration. During each subgoal execution, if no execution
error occurs, we consider the subgoal completed and proceed to the next one. Otherwise, we refine
the program until three rounds of code refinement (equivalent to four rounds of code generation)
are completed, and then move on to the next subgoal. If three consecutive subgoals do not result in
acquiring a new item, we replan by rerunning the task decomposition.

B.3
Ablations
We ablate 6 design choices (automatic curriculum, skill library, environment feedback, execution
errors, self-verification, and GPT-4 for code generation) in VOYAGER and study their impact on
exploration performance.
• Manual Curriculum: We substitute the automatic curriculum with a manually designed
curriculum for mining a diamond: “Mine 3 wood log”, “Craft 1 crafting table”, “Craft
1 wooden pickaxe”, “Mine 11 cobblestone”, “Craft 1 stone pickaxe”, “Craft 1 furnace”,
“Mine 3 iron ore”, “Smelt 3 iron ore”, “Craft 1 iron pickaxe”, “Mine 1 diamond”. A manual
curriculum requires human effort to design and is not scalable for open-ended exploration.
• Random Curriculum: We curate 101 items obtained by VOYAGER and create a random
curriculum by randomly selecting one item as the next task.
• w/o Skill Library: We remove the skill library, eliminating skill retrieval for code generation.
• w/o Environment Feedback: We exclude environment feedback (chat log) from the prompt
for code generation.
• w/o Execution Errors: We exclude execution errors from the prompt for code generation.
• w/o Self-Verification: For each task, we generate code without self-verification and it-
eratively refine the program for 3 rounds (equivalent to 4 rounds of code generation in
total).
• GPT-3.5: We replace GPT-4 with GPT-3.5 for code generation. We retain GPT-4 for the
automatic curriculum and the self-verification module.

Based on the following chunk summaries, provide a concise overall summary of the entire context. Summaries:
Video PreTraining (VPT) learns to act in sequential decision domains by watching large amounts of unlabeled online videos, using a small set of labeled data to train an inverse dynamics model (IDM) that labels actions in those videos. A non-causal IDM labels a vast unlabeled dataset (about 70k hours after filtering from ~270k hours of video), and a foundation model is then trained by behavioral cloning on these IDM-labeled examples, with the system demonstrated in Minecraft using the native mouse-and-keyboard interface at 20 Hz. The approach emphasizes data efficiency of the IDM, enables zero-shot capabilities, and can be fine-tuned with behavioral cloning or reinforcement learning to achieve complex tasks like crafting tools, illustrating efficient use of labeled contractor data within the VPT pipeline.
---
Fine-tuning the VPT foundation model on the contractor_house data massively expands crafting and tool-use behaviors (e.g., emergence of wooden and stone tools, more village raiding), far more so than earlygame_keyword fine-tuning. When combined with reinforcement learning and a KL-divergence constraint to prevent forgetting, the approach can master difficult sequences like obtaining a diamond pickaxe within 10 minutes, and a three-phase pipeline (pretraining, BC fine-tuning, RL fine-tuning) achieves human-level performance on iron pickaxes and mining diamonds. Data scaling results show that using labeled contractor data to train the IDM is far more data-efficient than direct BC training, with meaningful gains already from 10–100 hours of contractor data and larger scales enabling further tool-crafting and access to vast unlabeled data, albeit with diminishing returns beyond about 100 hours.
---
This section lists a wide range of related work on imitation learning, learning from demonstrations, and reinforcement learning with self-supervision and curriculum strategies. It then details how the authors collected Minecraft gameplay data: an initial internet dataset of around 270k hours, filtered down to a clean subset (web_clean) using MTurk-labeled frames and an RN50x64 CLIP-based embedding with an SVM classifier. An early_game subset is defined from web_clean to target early-game behavior. The authors also describe contractor data collection using a custom Minecraft recorder, yielding about 4,500 hours of gameplay (roughly $160k total cost) used to train an IDM and inform RL fine-tuning, including tasks like wood gathering, building a simple house, and obtaining a diamond pickaxe.
---
IDM training used about 1962 hours of data from general Minecraft gameplay and the treechop task, while contractor_house data (~420 hours) was reserved for behavioral cloning and not IDM training. The Minecraft environment is a MineRL/MCP-Reborn hybrid that renders 128x128 observations (with GUI overlays) and supports a large, human-like action space; null actions are filtered to reduce degenerate behavior. The IDM is a ~0.5B-parameter model that ingests 128-frame sequences, using a 3D temporal convolution, a ResNet62 backbone, and non-causal transformer blocks to predict per-frame actions, and it was trained for 20 epochs on 32 A100 GPUs with data augmentation; IDM-generated pseudo labels train the foundation model under a causal BC setup, with a hierarchical joint action space to better capture action dependencies.
---
The RL fine-tuning uses PPG with two sleep cycles and a KL divergence loss to a frozen pretrained policy to prevent forgetting, while optimizing the value and auxiliary value functions and using a reward structure that climbs a diamond-pickaxe ladder with tiered item rewards to shape behavior. The experiments also explore model scale, showing that larger models can outperform smaller ones after task-specific fine-tuning (e.g., contractor data), though smaller models may perform better zero-shot; the training runs involved massive compute and data (millions of episodes and billions of frames). In addition, the authors pretrain a language-conditioned agent by adding text conditioning from closed captions to a VPT foundation model, and find evidence of steerability—conditioning on exploration or other prompts changes travel distance, resource collection, and mining behavior.
---
The study tests how natural-language conditioning affects an agent’s steerability in Minecraft, showing that prompts like “explore” and “water” tend to increase movement, while others influence what the agent collects (e.g., seeds, wood, dirt). Despite some steerability, the authors find it to be weak and not yet practically useful, with crafting prompts not reliably changing outcomes and difficulties in tasks like gathering flowers or hunting; results rely on 95% confidence intervals over 1,000 episodes and significance is declared when bar overlaps do not occur. They then present VOYAGER, an open-ended, LLM-powered embodied lifelong learning agent with three components: an automatic curriculum to maximize exploration, a growing skill library of executable code, and an iterative prompting mechanism that uses environment feedback, execution errors, and self-verification. Empirically, VOYAGER outperforms baselines by 3.3× more unique items, 2.3× longer travel, and up to 15.3× faster tech milestones, and it can generalize to new worlds by reusing learned skills.
---
VOYAGER consistently outperforms baselines in exploration, discovering 63 novel items in 160 prompting iterations, masterfully unlocks the Minecraft tech tree (including diamond) faster, and traverses maps 2.3× longer across diverse terrains. It also demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks, solving all tasks with its skill library while baselines fail within 50 prompts, and the skill library can boost other methods like AutoGPT. Ablation studies show the automatic curriculum, the skill library, self-verification, and GPT-4 for code generation are all crucial, with self-verification being the most impactful. Human feedback can further assist VOYAGER in constructing complex 3D structures and guiding the curriculum, though limitations include GPT-4 cost and occasional inaccuracies and hallucinations.
---
The VOYAGER method coordinates task-driven code generation to act in a Minecraft-like environment using four agents: a curriculum agent (proposes tasks), an action agent (generates code), a critic (verifies success), and a skill manager (adds/retrieves skills), looping up to four rounds per task and updating the curriculum based on outcomes. The prompting section explains using GPT-4 or GPT-3.5 with system, user, and assistant roles, and a token-saving approach that concatenates prompts. The automatic curriculum details enumerate the prompt components (inventory, equipment, nearby blocks/entities, seen blocks, chests, biome, time, health, position, previous tasks, and additional context), and describe using chain-of-thought prompting and a self-ask mechanism with GPT-3.5 to retrieve relevant wiki knowledge, though the external knowledge base is optional.
---
This chunk describes an automatic curriculum for a Minecraft agent where the prompt gradually incorporates the agent’s state and context as it completes more tasks, using a predefined warm-up schedule that adds information like inventory, nearby blocks, position, biome, and health at increasing task counts. It then presents multiple full prompts (Prompt 1–3) that shape how the agent should select the next task, ask questions, or answer questions, with strict formatting, specificity, and constraints to keep tasks manageable and novel. It also details a Skill Library and extensive prompts (Prompt 4 and A.4.2) for generating Mineflayer-based code, including a suite of control APIs, environment feedback, and reusable helper functions, plus a required response format and step-by-step planning for code completion. Overall, the text outlines how prompts, state, exemplars, and code-generation templates work together to drive automatic curriculum-driven learning and action in Minecraft.
---
A
rlm_calls.response:
- The work presents a data-efficient pipeline for training embodied agents in sequential decision tasks (Minecraft) by leveraging Video PreTraining (VPT): an inverse dynamics model (IDM) is trained on large unlabeled video data to label actions, and a foundation model is trained by behavioral cloning on those labels, with optional reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning.
- Data and models: unlabeled data starts from ~270k hours of gameplay, filtered to a clean web subset (~70k hours after filtering); contractor data (~4.5k hours) is used to inform IDM and RL. IDM (~0.5B parameters) processes 128-frame sequences with a 3D conv, ResNet backbone, and non-causal transformers; IDM labeling enables data-efficient BC and RL in Minecraft at 20 Hz. Training used substantial compute (e.g., 32 A100 GPUs, 20 epochs) and involved a hierarchical action space.
- Results: fine-tuning with contractor data dramatically expands tool-use and crafting behavior (e.g., wooden/stone tools, iron/diamond tools) and combines with RL (PPG with KL-regularization) to reach human-level performance on complex tasks like obtaining a diamond pickaxe. Data scaling shows IDM-guided labeling is far more data-efficient than direct BC, with meaningful gains already 10–100 hours and larger scales enabling more advanced crafting, albeit with diminishing returns beyond ~100 hours.
- Language conditioning and steerability: adding natural-language conditioning to the VPT foundation shows limited, task-dependent steerability in Minecraft; prompts can bias exploration and resource gathering but are not reliably predictive. This motivates VOYAGER, an open-ended, LLM-powered embodied lifelong learning agent with four components: automatic curriculum, growing skill library, an executable-code action agent, and a self-verification critic.
- VOYAGER performance: VOYAGER substantially outperforms baselines in exploration (63 novel items in 160 prompts), faster progression through the Minecraft tech tree (including diamond) and longer, cross-terrain travel; it generalizes zero-shot to unseen worlds and can boost other methods when combined with its skill library. Ablation studies show the automatic curriculum, skill library, self-verification, and GPT-4/code generation capabilities as key contributors, with self-verification being particularly impactful.
- System architecture and prompts: the approach coordinates task-driven code generation via four agents (curriculum proposer, action coder, critic/verifier, and skill manager) in iterative rounds, guided by carefully designed prompts and a growing state context. The automatic curriculum progressively injects state information (inventory, position, biome, health, etc.) and uses multiple prompt templates to manage task selection, questions, and planning. A comprehensive Mineflayer-based code library and templates enable reusable control APIs and environment feedback loops, forming an end-to-end, prompt-driven, automatic curriculum for learning and acting in Minecraft.

prompt.content:
You are tasked with answering a query with associated context. You can access, transform, and analyze this context interactively in a REPL environment that can recursively query sub-LLMs, which you are strongly encouraged to use as much as possible. You will be queried iteratively until you provide a final answer.

The REPL environment is initialized with:
1. A `context` variable that contains extremely important information about your query. You should check the content of the `context` variable to understand what you are working with. Make sure you look through it sufficiently as you answer your query.
2. A `llm_query(prompt, model=None)` function that makes a single LLM completion call (no REPL, no iteration). Fast and lightweight -- use this for simple extraction, summarization, or Q&A over a chunk of text. The sub-LLM can handle around 500K chars.
3. A `llm_query_batched(prompts, model=None)` function that runs multiple `llm_query` calls concurrently: returns `List[str]` in the same order as input prompts. Much faster than sequential `llm_query` calls for independent queries.
4. A `rlm_query(prompt, model=None)` function that spawns a **recursive RLM sub-call** for deeper thinking subtasks. The child gets its own REPL environment and can reason iteratively over the prompt, just like you. Use this when a subtask requires multi-step reasoning, code execution, or its own iterative problem-solving -- not just a simple one-shot answer. Falls back to `llm_query` if recursion is not available.
5. A `rlm_query_batched(prompts, model=None)` function that spawns multiple recursive RLM sub-calls. Each prompt gets its own child RLM. Falls back to `llm_query_batched` if recursion is not available.
6. A `SHOW_VARS()` function that returns all variables you have created in the REPL. Use this to check what variables exist before using FINAL_VAR.
7. The ability to use `print()` statements to view the output of your REPL code and continue your reasoning.


**When to use `llm_query` vs `rlm_query`:**
- Use `llm_query` for simple, one-shot tasks: extracting info from a chunk, summarizing text, answering a factual question, classifying content. These are fast single LLM calls.
- Use `rlm_query` when the subtask itself requires deeper thinking: multi-step reasoning, solving a sub-problem that needs its own REPL and iteration, or tasks where a single LLM call might not be enough. The child RLM can write and run code, query further sub-LLMs, and iterate to find the answer.

**Breaking down problems:** You must break problems into more digestible components—whether that means chunking or summarizing a large context, or decomposing a hard task into easier sub-problems and delegating them via `llm_query` / `rlm_query`. Use the REPL to write a **programmatic strategy** that uses these LLM calls to solve the problem, as if you were building an agent: plan steps, branch on results, combine answers in code.

**REPL for computation:** You can also use the REPL to compute programmatic steps (e.g. `math.sin(x)`, distances, physics formulas) and then chain those results into an LLM call. For complex math or physics, compute intermediate quantities in code and pass the numbers to the LM for interpretation or the final answer. Example: data describes an electron in a magnetic field undergoing helical motion; task is to find the entry angle.
```repl
import math
# Suppose the context or an earlier LM call gave us: B, m, q, pitch, R (radius). Extract or set them.
# Helical motion: v_parallel = pitch * (q*B)/(2*pi*m), v_perp = R * (q*B)/m. Entry angle theta: tan(theta) = v_perp/v_parallel.
v_parallel = pitch * (q * B) / (2 * math.pi * m)
v_perp = R * (q * B) / m
theta_rad = math.atan2(v_perp, v_parallel)
theta_deg = math.degrees(theta_rad)
final_answer = llm_query(f"An electron entered a B field and underwent helical motion. Computed entry angle: {theta_deg:.2f} deg. State the answer clearly for the user.")
```
You will only be able to see truncated outputs from the REPL environment, so you should use the query LLM function on variables you want to analyze. You will find this function especially useful when you have to analyze the semantics of the context. Use these variables as buffers to build up your final answer.
Make sure to explicitly look through the entire context in REPL before answering your query. Break the context and the problem into digestible pieces: e.g. figure out a chunking strategy, break up the context into smart chunks, query an LLM per chunk and save answers to a buffer, then query an LLM over the buffers to produce your final answer.

You can use the REPL environment to help you understand your context, especially if it is huge. Remember that your sub LLMs are powerful -- they can fit around 500K characters in their context window, so don't be afraid to put a lot of context into them. For example, a viable strategy is to feed 10 documents per sub-LLM query. Analyze your input data and see if it is sufficient to just fit it in a few sub-LLM calls!

When you want to execute Python code in the REPL environment, wrap it in triple backticks with 'repl' language identifier. For example, say we want our recursive model to search for the magic number in the context (assuming the context is a string), and the context is very long, so we want to chunk it:
```repl
chunk = context[:10000]
answer = llm_query(f"What is the magic number in the context? Here is the chunk: {chunk}")
print(answer)
```

As an example, suppose you're trying to answer a question about a book. You can iteratively chunk the context section by section, query an LLM on that chunk, and track relevant information in a buffer.
```repl
query = "In Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone, did Gryffindor win the House Cup because they led?"
for i, section in enumerate(context):
    if i == len(context) - 1:
        buffer = llm_query(f"You are on the last section of the book. So far you know that: {buffers}. Gather from this last section to answer {query}. Here is the section: {section}")
        print(f"Based on reading iteratively through the book, the answer is: {buffer}")
    else:
        buffer = llm_query(f"You are iteratively looking through a book, and are on section {i} of {len(context)}. Gather information to help answer {query}. Here is the section: {section}")
        print(f"After section {i} of {len(context)}, you have tracked: {buffer}")
```

As another example, when the context isn't that long (e.g. >100M characters), a simple but viable strategy is, based on the context chunk lengths, to combine them and recursively query an LLM over chunks. For example, if the context is a List[str], we ask the same query over each chunk using `llm_query_batched` for concurrent processing:
```repl
query = "A man became famous for his book "The Great Gatsby". How many jobs did he have?"
# Suppose our context is ~1M chars, and we want each sub-LLM query to be ~0.1M chars so we split it into 10 chunks
chunk_size = len(context) // 10
chunks = []
for i in range(10):
    if i < 9:
        chunk_str = "
".join(context[i*chunk_size:(i+1)*chunk_size])
    else:
        chunk_str = "
".join(context[i*chunk_size:])
    chunks.append(chunk_str)

# Use batched query for concurrent processing - much faster than sequential calls!
prompts = [f"Try to answer the following query: {query}. Here are the documents:
{chunk}. Only answer if you are confident in your answer based on the evidence." for chunk in chunks]
answers = llm_query_batched(prompts)
for i, answer in enumerate(answers):
    print(f"I got the answer from chunk {i}: {answer}")
final_answer = llm_query(f"Aggregating all the answers per chunk, answer the original query about total number of jobs: {query}\n\nAnswers:\n" + "\n".join(answers))
```

For subtasks that require deeper reasoning (e.g. solving a complex sub-problem), use `rlm_query` instead. The child gets its own REPL to iterate; you can then use the result in parent logic:
```repl
# Child RLM solves the sub-problem in its own REPL; we use the result in code
trend = rlm_query(f"Analyze this dataset and conclude with one word: up, down, or stable: {data}")
if "up" in trend.lower():
    recommendation = "Consider increasing exposure."
elif "down" in trend.lower():
    recommendation = "Consider hedging."
else:
    recommendation = "Hold position."
```

Global summary: - The work presents a data-efficient pipeline for training embodied agents in sequential decision tasks (Minecraft) by leveraging Video PreTraining (VPT): an inverse dynamics model (IDM) is trained on large unlabeled video data to label actions, and a foundation model is trained by behavioral cloning on those labels, with optional reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning.
- Data and models: unlabeled data starts from ~270k hours of gameplay, filtered to a clean web subset (~70k hours after filtering); contractor data (~4.5k hours) is used to inform IDM and RL. IDM (~0.5B parameters) processes 128-frame sequences with a 3D conv, ResNet backbone, and non-causal transformers; IDM labeling enables data-efficient BC and RL in Minecraft at 20 Hz. Training used substantial compute (e.g., 32 A100 GPUs, 20 epochs) and involved a hierarchical action space.
- Results: fine-tuning with contractor data dramatically expands tool-use and crafting behavior (e.g., wooden/stone tools, iron/diamond tools) and combines with RL (PPG with KL-regularization) to reach human-level performance on complex tasks like obtaining a diamond pickaxe. Data scaling shows IDM-guided labeling is far more data-efficient than direct BC, with meaningful gains already 10–100 hours and larger scales enabling more advanced crafting, albeit with diminishing returns beyond ~100 hours.
- Language conditioning and steerability: adding natural-language conditioning to the VPT foundation shows limited, task-dependent steerability in Minecraft; prompts can bias exploration and resource gathering but are not reliably predictive. This motivates VOYAGER, an open-ended, LLM-powered embodied lifelong learning agent with four components: automatic curriculum, growing skill library, an executable-code action agent, and a self-verification critic.
- VOYAGER performance: VOYAGER substantially outperforms baselines in exploration (63 novel items in 160 prompts), faster progression through the Minecraft tech tree (including diamond) and longer, cross-terrain travel; it generalizes zero-shot to unseen worlds and can boost other methods when combined with its skill library. Ablation studies show the automatic curriculum, skill library, self-verification, and GPT-4/code generation capabilities as key contributors, with self-verification being particularly impactful.
- System architecture and prompts: the approach coordinates task-driven code generation via four agents (curriculum proposer, action coder, critic/verifier, and skill manager) in iterative rounds, guided by carefully designed prompts and a growing state context. The automatic curriculum progressively injects state information (inventory, position, biome, health, etc.) and uses multiple prompt templates to manage task selection, questions, and planning. A comprehensive Mineflayer-based code library and templates enable reusable control APIs and environment feedback loops, forming an end-to-end, prompt-driven, automatic curriculum for learning and acting in Minecraft.

- Topic: VOYAGER-style embodied agent research in Minecraft, focusing on data-efficient training by combining unlabeled video data, inverse dynamics modeling (IDM), behavioral cloning (BC), and reinforcement learning (RL), plus a novel four-agent system for automatic curriculum and control.

- Core idea: Leverage large-scale unlabeled gameplay data to train an IDM that labels actions from video, then use those labels to train a foundation model via BC, with optional RL fine-tuning. This data-efficient pipeline enables sophisticated tool-use and crafting behaviors in a Minecraft-like environment.

- Data and models:
  - Unlabeled data: ~270k hours of gameplay, filtered to a clean web subset (~70k hours).
  - Contractor data: ~4.5k hours to inform IDM and RL.
  - IDM: ~0.5B parameters, processes 128-frame sequences with a 3D conv, ResNet backbone, and non-causal transformers; used to label actions for BC.
  - Training setup: substantial compute (e.g., 32 A100 GPUs), 20 epochs, BC at ~20 Hz, with hierarchical action spaces.

- Training strategy and results:
  - Data-efficient labeling via IDM enables BC and subsequent RL with KL-regularization (PPG).
  - Fine-tuning with contractor data significantly expands tool-use and crafting capabilities (e.g., wooden/stone tools, progression toward iron/diamond tools) and accelerates progress through the Minecraft tech tree.
  - Data scaling shows IDM-guided labeling is far more data-efficient than direct BC, with meaningful gains starting at tens of hours and larger gains at higher scales, though returns diminish beyond roughly 100 hours.

- Language conditioning and steerability:
  - Adding natural-language conditioning to the VPT foundation yields limited, task-dependent steerability; prompts can bias exploration and resource gathering but are not reliably predictive.
  - This motivates VOYAGER, a four-component embodied lifelong learning system designed to be more controllable and adaptable.

- System architecture (four agents):
  - Curriculum proposer: generates tasks and planning prompts.
  - Action coder: writes executable code to act in the environment.
  - Critic/verifier: evaluates actions and outcomes for quality and safety.
  - Skill manager: maintains and grows a library of acquired skills.
  - The system uses an automatic curriculum, growing skill library, executable-code action generation, and self-verification to improve performance over time.

- Implementation details:
  - Automatic curriculum injects state information (inventory, position, biome, health, etc.) into prompts and uses templates to manage task selection, questions, and planning.
  - A Mineflayer-based code library provides reusable control APIs and feedback loops, forming an end-to-end, prompt-driven workflow for learning and acting in Minecraft.

- Takeaways for practice:
  - A data-efficient pipeline that couples unlabeled video supervision with small, targeted labeled data can drive substantial behavioral and skill improvements in embodied agents.
  - A modular, four-agent architecture with an automatic curriculum and self-verification can improve robustness and generalization, while language conditioning remains a useful but limited tool for steerability.
  - When designing similar systems, consider: scalable unlabeled data, a strong labeling model (IDM) to convert videos into useful supervision, a robust BC/RL fusion strategy, and an interpretable, modular control architecture.
